evidence.scientific_clarity: Dual-axis log-loss + cosine LR exactly matches brief; color-coupled axis ticks/labels implemented as hinted; title and axis labels are unambiguous.
evidence.typography: No pdf.fonttype=42 or svg.fonttype='none' set anywhere in script; font family not overridden so defaults to DejaVu Sans; font sizes (11/12pt) are otherwise reasonable.
evidence.palette: Blue (#1f77b4) and red (#d62728) are distinguishable under deuteranopia/protanopia; no green/red directional confusion; but these are just matplotlib defaults, not a deliberate colorblind-optimized palette.
evidence.layout_density: tight_layout() used, figsize=(6.5,3.8) is sensible, xlim pinned to data range, LR y-axis bottom=0; no gaping whitespace or label collisions evident.
evidence.reviewer_readiness: Caption correctly notes axes measure different quantities and matches figure content; legend placed upper-right may overlap high early-loss region; no explicit x-axis range stated in caption.
evidence.vector_fidelity: pdf.fonttype=42 is absent from the script; PDF will embed Type 3 (bitmap) fonts, making text non-selectable and non-editable in Illustrator/Inkscape.
evidence.file_format: figure.pdf, figure.png, figure.svg, caption.tex, and gen_figure.py all present and non-trivial; exceeds minimum requirements.
evidence.caption_quality: Caption provides specific numerical claims (loss 2.19→0.21, LR 10^-3→0), explains color-coupling of axes, and explicitly warns reader that the two axes are not directly comparable.

overall_strengths: Caption is exemplary: numerical claims, color-coding explanation, and a reader-warning about dual-axis comparability are all present.
All required output files exist (pdf, png, svg, caption.tex, gen_figure.py) and the layout is clean with tight_layout and color-coupled axis labels matching the brief's hints.
overall_weaknesses: Missing matplotlib.rcParams['pdf.fonttype'] = 42 and svg.fonttype='none' — PDF/SVG text will be bitmapped Type 3 fonts, failing vector-fidelity and editability requirements.
Font family is never set, so the figure uses matplotlib's DejaVu Sans default rather than a publication-appropriate typeface (e.g. Helvetica, Arial, or a serif matching the journal template).
